exemplar.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Clear, sustained focus on the prescribed title and consistent return to Boxâs aphorism.
Effective linkage to two Areas of Knowledge (Mathematics and the Human Sciences) supported by well-chosen real-world and theoretical examples.
Logical structure and use of visual aids (e.g. SolowâSwan diagram) to reinforce argumentation.
Accurate definitions of key concepts (model-building, approximation, usefulness, accuracy) that anchor the discussion.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive; critical evaluation of underlying assumptions and counter-claims is underdeveloped.
Knowledge questions are not explicitly signposted, leading to occasional lapses in coherence and direction.
Alternative perspectives (utilitarian vs. precisionist) are acknowledged but not systematically compared or weighed.
Typographical and conceptual slips (e.g. âamorphismâ for âaphorismâ) undermine precision and academic rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The total mark of 4 reflects a submission that meets many of the band-5 descriptorsâclear focus on the title, effective use of two AOKs, coherent examples and structured argumentâwhile falling short in critical analysis and evaluation. Strand assessments highlight strong contextual understanding and organization (justifying an above-average share of the available marks) but also identify pervasive weaknesses in depth of analysis, signposting of knowledge questions and rigorous evaluation of alternative viewpoints. To reconcile the official total of 4, the essayâs clear strengths in focus and structure are acknowledged, but significant penalties are applied for its predominantly explanatory approach, lack of sustained critical engagement and lapses in academic precision.

annotations.comment: The treatment of Maslowâs model overlooks cross-cultural critiques and empirical challenges, limiting the evaluation.
The discussion of Eulerâs method lacks quantitative analysis of error propagation; including error bounds like $$O(h)$$ would enhance rigor.
The transition to an alternate interpretation of âcorrectâ is promising but underdeveloped; clearer linkage to Boxâs claim is needed.
The conclusion that models are âcorrectâ if they approximately represent phenomena requires a precise definition of âcorrectness.â
The real-world applications of Maslowâs hierarchy in marketing and education demonstrate effective use of human sciences examples.
The discussion of historical models stops short of critical evaluation; the student should examine specific counterexamples where old models misled research.
This opening statement accurately restates the prescribed title, ensuring focus and clarity for the essayâs line of inquiry.
The term âamorphismâ appears to be a typographical error for âaphorism,â which could confuse the intended meaning of Boxâs phrase.
The introduction outlines areas of knowledge but lacks clearly formulated knowledge questions; the student should signpost specific questions to guide the analysis.
The inclusion of Figure 2 effectively visualizes the SolowâSwan model, supporting the discussion of capital accumulation and steady state.
The narrative describing the SolowâSwan model is largely descriptive; the student should compare its predictive precision with that of predecessor models.
The assertion that the model is âwrongâ because it uses only three variables is simplistic; the student should explore the consequences of omitting relevant factors.
The use of Eulerâs method as an example of mathematical approximation aptly illustrates the theme of useful yet imperfect models.
The sinusoidal regression example is illustrative but lacks discussion of fitting challenges such as overfitting and parameter sensitivity.
The distinction between utilitarian and precisionist perspectives clarifies different criteria for evaluating model usefulness.
The discussion of utilitarian versus precisionist views remains abstract; concrete AOK examples illustrating each perspective would strengthen the analysis.
When referencing Lorenzâs butterfly effect, the citation should include precise source details (e.g., page numbers) to improve academic rigor.
The explanation of the inductionâdeduction feedback loop is clear and demonstrates understanding of Boxâs theoretical framework.
The diagram is described but not critically assessed; the student should analyze how this feedback loopâs limitations affect the usefulness of models.
The SolowâSwan model description omits a critique of its exogenous technology assumption; discussing this limitation would deepen the analysis.
Tracing the SolowâSwan modelâs evolution from HarrodâDomar demonstrates clear understanding of historical model progression in the human sciences.
Acknowledging that a modelâs inspiration can render it useful despite inaccuracies reflects an insightful appreciation of scientific progress.
The paragraph on Boxâs conception of âwrongâ could benefit from concrete examples illustrating the tension between simulation and representation.
The theoretical framework of inductionâdeduction is not linked back to a mathematics example; relating it to Eulerâs method would strengthen coherence.
The caption restates the diagram without analysis; the student should interpret the modelâs implications for the essayâs central claim.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
